title: Casanare Department

Santander](/wiki/Norte_de_Santander_Department "Norte de Santander Department")   [Putumayo](/wiki/Putumayo_Department "Putumayo Department")   [Quindío](/wiki/Quind%C3%ADo_Department "Quindío Department") | [Risaralda](/wiki/Risaralda_Department "Risaralda Department")   [San Andrés](/wiki/Archipelago_of_San_Andr%C3%A9s,_Providencia_and_Santa_Catalina "Archipelago of San Andrés, Providencia and Santa Catalina")   [Santander](/wiki/Santander_Department "Santander Department")   [Sucre](/wiki/Sucre_Department "Sucre Department")   [Tolima](/wiki/Tolima_Department "Tolima Department")   [Valle del Cauca](/wiki/Valle_del_Cauca_Department "Valle del Cauca Department") | [Vaupés](/wiki/Vaup%C3%A9s_Department "Vaupés Department")   [Vichada](/wiki/Vichada_Department "Vichada Department")    Capital district:   [Bogotá](/wiki/Bogot%C3%A1 "Bogotá") | |

(Spanish pronunciation: [[kasaˈnaɾe]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish"), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Departamento de Casanare*) is a [department](/wiki/Departments_of_Colombia "Departments of Colombia") located in the central eastern of [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia"). It is famous for its oil and natural gas production as well as its livestock and extensive plains.[[4]](#cite_note-4) It is also the tenth largest department with an area of 44,490 km2, similar to that of [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"), but also the seventh least densely populated.

["DANE"](https://web.archive.org/web/20091113143414/http://www.dane.gov.co/daneweb_V09/index.php?option=com_content&view=article&id=75&Itemid=72). Archived from [the original](http://www.dane.gov.co/daneweb_V09/index.php?option=com_content&view=article&id=75&Itemid=72) on November 13, 2009. Retrieved February 13, 2013.
["Sub-national HDI - Area Database - Global Data Lab"](https://hdi.globaldatalab.org/areadata/shdi/). *hdi.globaldatalab.org*. Retrieved 2018-09-13.

S.A.S, Editorial La República. ["Campos petroleros de Casanare, los que más aportaron al aumento de la producción"](https://www.larepublica.co/economia/campos-petroleros-de-casanare-los-que-mas-aportaron-al-aumento-de-la-produccion-3418252). *Diario La República* (in Spanish). Retrieved 2023-09-12.

["Archived copy"](https://web.archive.org/web/20090327183137/http://www.ingetec.com.co/brochures-ingles/BROCHURE-PRESAS-INGLES.pdf) (PDF). Archived from [the original](http://www.ingetec.com.co/brochures-ingles/BROCHURE-PRESAS-INGLES.pdf) (PDF) on 2009-03-27. Retrieved 2008-09-16.`{{cite web}}`: CS1 maint: archived copy as title ([link](/wiki/Category:CS1_maint:_archived_copy_as_title "Category:CS1 maint: archived copy as title"))

["Reloj de Población"](https://web.archive.org/web/20180116020433/http://www.dane.gov.co/reloj/). *DANE*. Departamento Administrativo Nacional de Estadísitica. Archived from [the original](http://www.dane.gov.co/reloj/) on 16 January 2018. Retrieved 6 July 2017.

| Website | [www.casanare.gov.co](http://www.casanare.gov.co/) |

**Casanare** (Spanish pronunciation: [[kasaˈnaɾe]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish"), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Departamento de Casanare*) is a [department](/wiki/Departments_of_Colombia "Departments of Colombia") located in the central eastern of [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia"). It is famous for its oil and natural gas production as well as its livestock and extensive plains.[[4]](#cite_note-4) It is also the tenth largest department with an area of 44,490 km2, similar to that of [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark"), but also the seventh least densely populated.

Its capital is [Yopal](/wiki/Yopal "Yopal"), which is also the episcopal seat of the [Roman Catholic Diocese of Yopal](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Yopal "Roman Catholic Diocese of Yopal").

It contains [oil fields](/wiki/Oil_field "Oil field") and an 800 km [pipeline](/wiki/Pipeline_transport "Pipeline transport") leading to the coastal port of [Coveñas](/wiki/Cove%C3%B1as "Coveñas") owned by [BP](/wiki/BP "BP").

## Rivers and dams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Casanare_Department&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Rivers and dams")]

The [Upía River](/wiki/Up%C3%ADa_River "Upía River") (Río Upía) is in Casanare.[[5]](#cite_note-5)
Casanare, Ariporo, Guachiría, Guanapalo, Pauto, Tocaría, Cravo Sur, Cusiana, Túa y Upía.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Casanare_Department&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Historical population

| Year | Pop. | ±% |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1973 | - | — |
| 1985 | 147,472 | — |
| 1993 | 211,329 | +43.3% |
| 2005 | 295,353 | +39.8% |
| 2018 | 420,504 | +42.4% |
| Source:[[6]](#cite_note-6) | | |

A former subregion of Boyacá, Casanare became a separate department in 1973.

## Flag

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Casanare_Department&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Flag")]

The flag is divided diagonally from the upper-fly corner to the lower-hoist corner. The upper-hoist triangle is red and the lower-fly triangle, green. At the center of the flag is an eight pointed sun in yellow. The meanings attributed to the elements are that the color red symbolizes the blood spilled by its heroes, the color green its natural resources and its prairies, and the sun has eight corners that represent each letter of the word CASANARE.[[7]](#cite_note-7)
